concept.title: How markdown-validator fits into your CI/CD pipeline
concept.shortdesc: When markdown-validator runs in your CI/CD pipeline, it acts as a quality gate on every pull request. If any Required rule fails, the pipeline exits with a non-zero code and blocks the merge until you fix the reported issues.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Learn how markdown-validator runs as a CI/CD gate to block pull requests that fail Required validation rules, and what happens when it finds failures.

section.title: How it works

image.alt: Flowchart: author pushes a commit, CI checks out the branch, installs markdown-validator, runs md-validate validate, then branches on exit code. Exit 0 means the PR is eligible to merge. Exit 1 blocks the merge; the author reads the failure report, fixes the file, pushes, and the pipeline re-runs.

ol.li: Push a commit or open a pull request.
CI runs md-validate validate docs/ --rules rules/tutorial.json (exact paths vary — check .github/workflows/validate-docs.yml).
Exit 0: all Required rules pass — PR is eligible to merge.
Exit 1: a Required rule failed — merge is blocked. Fix the failures shown in the log, push again, pipeline re-runs.

section.note: Run validation locally before opening a pull request to catch failures before CI does. Use the same --rules path that CI uses — both must point to the same committed file, or results will differ.

section.title: Set up CI
section.p: See Add markdown-validator to a GitHub Actions workflow.
